questions.stem: The basic reproduction number $R_0$ is defined as
options: the total number of people in the population
the average number of secondary infections caused by one infective in a completely susceptible population
the rate at which infectives recover
the time until the epidemic peaks
questions.explanation: $R_0$ is the average number of secondary infections produced by a single infective introduced into a wholly susceptible population. It is a dimensionless threshold quantity, not a population count, a recovery rate, or a timing. This single number determines whether an introduced infection will grow into an outbreak or fade out.
questions.source: in the style of Hethcote / Brauer and Castillo-Chavez, Mathematical Models in Population Biology and Epidemiology

questions.stem: For the basic SIR model, the basic reproduction number is
questions.explanation: From the condition $\frac{dI}{dt} \geq 0$, the SIR model gives $\frac{\beta S}{\gamma} \geq 1$, and evaluating at the initial susceptible level yields $R_0 = \frac{\beta S_0}{\gamma}$. It is the transmission rate times the initial susceptibles divided by the recovery rate. The reciprocal and the product forms do not match the derived threshold. The correct expression is $\beta S_0/\gamma$.
questions.source: in the style of Hethcote / Brauer and Castillo-Chavez, Mathematical Models in Population Biology and Epidemiology

questions.stem: According to the threshold theorem, an epidemic will take off only if
questions.explanation: The threshold theorem states that the epidemic will not get started unless the initial number of susceptibles exceeds a threshold, equivalently $R_0 > 1$. When $R_0 < 1$ the disease dies out, and $R_0 = 0$ would mean no transmission at all. Equality of $\beta$ and $\gamma$ is not the general threshold condition. An outbreak requires $R_0$ above one.
questions.source: in the style of Hethcote / Brauer and Castillo-Chavez, Mathematical Models in Population Biology and Epidemiology

questions.stem: If $R_0 < 1$ for a disease introduced into a susceptible population, then
options: the disease dies out with no sustained transmission
the number of infectives grows without bound
questions.explanation: When $R_0 < 1$, on average each infective fails to replace itself, so the chain of transmission breaks and the disease dies out. It does not grow into a pandemic, increase without bound, or infect everyone. Sustained spread requires $R_0$ to exceed one. Below one, infections decline to extinction.
questions.source: in the style of Hethcote / Brauer and Castillo-Chavez, Mathematical Models in Population Biology and Epidemiology

questions.stem: The condition $R_0 = 1$ is the threshold separating
options: die out from spread, the critical balance where each infective replaces itself exactly once
the latent period from the infectious period
questions.explanation: At $R_0 = 1$ each infective on average transmits to exactly one susceptible, so the disease neither grows nor disappears. This is the critical threshold dividing extinction ($R_0 < 1$) from epidemic growth ($R_0 > 1$). It is not a statement about population sizes, disease stages, or vital rates. The value one is the tipping point of transmission.
questions.source: in the style of Hethcote / Brauer and Castillo-Chavez, Mathematical Models in Population Biology and Epidemiology

questions.stem: Starting from $\dfrac{dI}{dt} \geq 0$ for the SIR model, which inequality leads to the threshold?
questions.explanation: The infective equation is $\frac{dI}{dt} = \beta S I - \gamma I$. Requiring it to be nonnegative gives $\beta S I \geq \gamma I$, and dividing by $\gamma I$ yields $\frac{\beta S}{\gamma} \geq 1$. Comparing with $R_0 \geq 1$ identifies $R_0 = \frac{\beta S}{\gamma}$. The other lines use a wrong sign or invert the ratio. The growth condition is $\frac{\beta S}{\gamma} \geq 1$.
questions.source: in the style of Hethcote / Brauer and Castillo-Chavez, Mathematical Models in Population Biology and Epidemiology

questions.stem: At the very start of an epidemic with $S_0 \approx N$, the SIR basic reproduction number can be written
questions.explanation: Because $S_0 + I_0 = N$ with a small initial infective seed, $S_0 \approx N$, so $R_0 = \frac{\beta S_0}{\gamma} \approx \frac{\beta N}{\gamma}$. The expression keeps $\beta N$ on top and $\gamma$ below. The distractors swap or invert the parameters. At onset the reproduction number is $\frac{\beta N}{\gamma}$.
questions.source: in the style of Hethcote / Brauer and Castillo-Chavez, Mathematical Models in Population Biology and Epidemiology

questions.stem: For the rescaled SIRS model with incidence $\beta s i$, the basic reproduction number is
questions.explanation: When the populations are rescaled to fractions so the incidence is $\beta s i$, the endemic equilibrium exists precisely when $R_0 = \frac{\beta}{\gamma} > 1$. So the reproduction number in those scaled units is $\frac{\beta}{\gamma}$. The reciprocal and product forms are wrong, and $\delta$ is the loss of immunity rate, not part of $R_0$ here. The scaled threshold is $\frac{\beta}{\gamma}$.
questions.source: in the style of Hethcote / Brauer and Castillo-Chavez, Mathematical Models in Population Biology and Epidemiology

questions.stem: A disease has transmission rate $\beta = 0.6$ per day and recovery rate $\gamma = 0.3$ per day (rescaled model). Its basic reproduction number is
questions.explanation: Compute $R_0 = \frac{\beta}{\gamma} = \frac{0.6}{0.3} = 2$. Since $R_0 = 2 > 1$, the disease spreads. The value $0.5$ inverts the ratio, and $0.18$ multiplies instead of dividing. The basic reproduction number is $2$.
questions.source: in the style of Hethcote / Brauer and Castillo-Chavez, Mathematical Models in Population Biology and Epidemiology

questions.stem: In an SIR model with $\beta = 0.001$ per day, $\gamma = 0.5$ per day, and $N = 2000$, the basic reproduction number at onset is
questions.explanation: Use $R_0 = \frac{\beta N}{\gamma} = \frac{0.001 \times 2000}{0.5} = \frac{2}{0.5} = 4$. With $R_0 = 4$ the disease will spread strongly. The value $0.25$ inverts the expression. The reproduction number is $4$.
questions.source: in the style of Hethcote / Brauer and Castillo-Chavez, Mathematical Models in Population Biology and Epidemiology

questions.stem: A disease has an infectious period averaging $14$ days, and an average infective makes adequate contact with $0.1$ susceptibles per day. Taking $R_0$ as contacts per day times infectious period, $R_0$ is
questions.explanation: The number of secondary infections is the contact rate times the duration of infectiousness: $R_0 = 0.1 \times 14 = 1.4$. Since $R_0 = 1.4 > 1$, the disease can spread. The value $0.14$ divides rather than multiplies. The reproduction number is $1.4$.
questions.source: in the style of Hethcote / Brauer and Castillo-Chavez, Mathematical Models in Population Biology and Epidemiology

questions.stem: For the disease in the previous setup ($R_0 = 1.4$ from contact rate $0.1$ per day and a $14$ day infectious period), to what level must the contact rate be reduced so the disease dies out?
options: below $0.1$ per day, any reduction will do
questions.explanation: The disease dies out when $R_0 < 1$, that is when (contact rate) $\times 14 < 1$, so the contact rate must fall below $\frac{1}{14} \approx 0.0714$ per day. Merely reducing below the current $0.1$ is not enough unless it drops past this critical value. The threshold contact rate is about $0.0714$ per day.
questions.source: in the style of Hethcote / Brauer and Castillo-Chavez, Mathematical Models in Population Biology and Epidemiology

questions.stem: The next-generation-matrix method expresses the basic reproduction number as
options: $R_0 = \rho\big(F V^{-1}\big)$, the spectral radius of $F V^{-1}$
$R_0 = F V$, the matrix product itself
questions.explanation: In the next generation method $R_0$ is the spectral radius (largest absolute eigenvalue) of the next generation matrix $F V^{-1}$, written $\rho(F V^{-1})$. It is not the determinant, a trace, or the raw matrix product. The dominant eigenvalue of $F V^{-1}$ is the reproduction number.
questions.source: in the style of Hethcote / Brauer and Castillo-Chavez, Mathematical Models in Population Biology and Epidemiology

questions.stem: In the next-generation-matrix construction, the matrix $F$ collects
options: the total population in each compartment
the rates of appearance of new infections in each infected compartment
the birth and death rates of susceptibles
questions.explanation: $F$ is built from the new infection terms, the rates at which new infections enter each infected compartment, evaluated at the disease free equilibrium. The remaining transfer terms (progression, recovery, death) go into $V$. $F$ does not hold population totals or vital rates of susceptibles. It is the new infection part of the linearized infected subsystem.
questions.source: in the style of Hethcote / Brauer and Castillo-Chavez, Mathematical Models in Population Biology and Epidemiology

questions.stem: In the next-generation-matrix method, the matrix $V$ contains
options: the transfer terms: progression, recovery, and removal between or out of infected compartments
questions.explanation: $V$ gathers the remaining transitions of the infected subsystem: movement between infected stages and removal through recovery or death, everything except brand new infections. The new infections themselves are in $F$. $V$ is not a population size or the spectral radius. It is the transfer matrix whose inverse weights $F$.
questions.source: in the style of Hethcote / Brauer and Castillo-Chavez, Mathematical Models in Population Biology and Epidemiology

questions.stem: When is the next-generation-matrix method especially needed rather than the elementary $\dfrac{dI}{dt} \geq 0$ argument?
options: when there are two or more infected compartments, such as exposed and infectious
when the population is exactly constant
when $R_0$ is already known to be one
when there is only a single infective class
questions.explanation: The simple threshold argument works for a single infective class, but real models such as SEIR have several infected compartments (exposed and infectious). The next generation method is applicable precisely when the number of infected classes is two or more, handling the coupling between them. A constant population or a known $R_0$ value is not the trigger. Multiple infected stages call for the matrix method.
questions.source: in the style of Hethcote / Brauer and Castillo-Chavez, Mathematical Models in Population Biology and Epidemiology

questions.stem: For the SEIR model without vital dynamics, the infected subsystem is $\dfrac{dE}{dt} = \beta S I - k E,\ \dfrac{dI}{dt} = k E - \gamma I$. Evaluated at the disease free equilibrium ($S = N$), the new infection matrix is
questions.explanation: The only new infection term is $\beta S I$, entering the exposed equation. Its derivative with respect to $I$ at $S = N$ is $\beta N$, and there is no new infection inflow to the $I$ equation. So $F = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & \beta N \\ 0 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$. The third matrix in the list is the transfer matrix $V$, not $F$, and the others place $\beta N$ in the wrong entry.
questions.source: in the style of Hethcote / Brauer and Castillo-Chavez, Mathematical Models in Population Biology and Epidemiology

questions.stem: Continuing the SEIR next-generation calculation, the transfer matrix is $V = \begin{pmatrix} k & 0 \\ -k & \gamma \end{pmatrix}$. The resulting reproduction number $\rho(F V^{-1})$ is
questions.explanation: Here $\det V = k\gamma$, so $V^{-1} = \frac{1}{k\gamma}\begin{pmatrix} \gamma & 0 \\ k & k \end{pmatrix}$. Then $F V^{-1} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & \beta N \\ 0 & 0 \end{pmatrix} V^{-1} = \begin{pmatrix} \frac{\beta N}{\gamma} & \frac{\beta N}{\gamma} \\ 0 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$. Its eigenvalues are $\frac{\beta N}{\gamma}$ and $0$, so the spectral radius is $\frac{\beta N}{\gamma}$. This matches the SIR value, since every exposed individual eventually becomes infectious.
questions.source: in the style of Hethcote / Brauer and Castillo-Chavez, Mathematical Models in Population Biology and Epidemiology

questions.stem: A single infective is introduced into a fully susceptible population and on average causes $3$ new infections before recovering. The basic reproduction number is
questions.explanation: By definition $R_0$ is the average number of secondary infections from one case in a fully susceptible population, which is exactly $3$ here. Since $R_0 = 3 > 1$, the disease will spread. The other values misread the definition. The reproduction number is $3$.
questions.source: in the style of Hethcote / Brauer and Castillo-Chavez, Mathematical Models in Population Biology and Epidemiology

questions.stem: Two diseases circulate in the same town: disease A has $R_0 = 1.2$ and disease B has $R_0 = 4$. Which statement is correct?
options: Only A can cause an epidemic
Both can cause epidemics, but B spreads more explosively than A
questions.explanation: Both exceed the threshold $R_0 = 1$, so both can cause epidemics, but a larger $R_0$ means each case generates more secondary infections, so disease B with $R_0 = 4$ spreads far more explosively than A with $R_0 = 1.2$. It is not true that only A spreads, that neither spreads, or that the smaller $R_0$ spreads faster. The higher reproduction number drives faster growth.
questions.source: in the style of Hethcote / Brauer and Castillo-Chavez, Mathematical Models in Population Biology and Epidemiology

questions.stem: Why does the basic reproduction number $R_0$ require the population to be completely susceptible in its definition?
options: Because prior immunity would reduce the secondary infections below the maximal potential the number is meant to capture
Because the population must be infinite
questions.explanation: $R_0$ is defined in a fully susceptible population so that it reflects the maximal transmission potential of the pathogen. If some individuals were already immune, contacts with them would not produce new infections, lowering the count below $R_0$. That reduced quantity is the effective reproduction number, not $R_0$. Immune individuals do not transmit more, $R_0$ concerns transmission, and the population need not be infinite.
questions.source: in the style of Hethcote / Brauer and Castillo-Chavez, Mathematical Models in Population Biology and Epidemiology

questions.stem: Holding $\beta$ and $N$ fixed in $R_0 = \dfrac{\beta N}{\gamma}$, increasing the recovery rate $\gamma$ (shortening the infectious period) has what effect on $R_0$?
questions.explanation: Since $\gamma$ sits in the denominator, raising it lowers $R_0$. A faster recovery means each infective spends less time transmitting, producing fewer secondary cases. So shortening the infectious period reduces the reproduction number. It cannot increase or leave it unchanged, and $R_0$ stays positive. Faster recovery drives $R_0$ down.
questions.source: in the style of Hethcote / Brauer and Castillo-Chavez, Mathematical Models in Population Biology and Epidemiology

questions.stem: Compute and classify a reproduction number for a rescaled model.
questions.solution: A rescaled SIRS model has transmission rate $\beta = 0.8$ per day and recovery rate $\gamma = 0.5$ per day. Its basic reproduction number is $R_0 = \beta/\gamma =$ {{1}}. Because this value is {{2}} one, the disease will spread and an endemic equilibrium exists. If $\gamma$ were increased to $0.8$ per day, $R_0$ would equal {{3}}.
questions.explanation: Here $R_0 = \beta/\gamma = 0.8/0.5 = 1.6 > 1$, so the disease spreads and an endemic state exists. Raising $\gamma$ to $0.8$ gives $R_0 = 0.8/0.8 = 1$, exactly the threshold.
questions.source: in the style of Hethcote / Brauer and Castillo-Chavez, Mathematical Models in Population Biology and Epidemiology

questions.stem: Apply the SIR onset formula and the threshold theorem.
questions.solution: An SIR disease has $\beta = 0.002$ per day, $\gamma = 0.4$ per day, in a population of $N = 1000$. At onset $R_0 = \beta N/\gamma =$ {{1}}. Since this exceeds one, the disease {{2}}. The smallest susceptible level $\gamma/\beta$ below which infectives can no longer grow equals {{3}}.
questions.explanation: At onset $R_0 = \beta N/\gamma = 0.002 \times 1000 / 0.4 = 2/0.4 = 5 > 1$, so the disease spreads. The peak threshold is $\gamma/\beta = 0.4/0.002 = 200$ susceptibles.
questions.source: in the style of Hethcote / Brauer and Castillo-Chavez, Mathematical Models in Population Biology and Epidemiology

questions.stem: State the next-generation-matrix recipe.
questions.solution: In the next generation method the matrix {{1}} holds the new infection terms and the matrix $V$ holds the remaining transfer terms, both evaluated at the disease free equilibrium. The basic reproduction number is the {{2}} of the matrix $F V^{-1}$. This method is required when the number of infected compartments is {{3}} or more.
questions.explanation: $F$ contains the new infection rates and $V$ the transfer rates. The reproduction number is $R_0 = \rho(F V^{-1})$, the spectral radius of the next generation matrix. The method is used when there are two or more infected compartments.
questions.source: in the style of Hethcote / Brauer and Castillo-Chavez, Mathematical Models in Population Biology and Epidemiology

questions.stem: Carry out the SEIR next-generation computation.
questions.solution: For the SEIR model without vital dynamics, at the disease free equilibrium $F = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & \beta N \\ 0 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$ and $V = \begin{pmatrix} k & 0 \\ -k & \gamma \end{pmatrix}$. The determinant of $V$ is {{1}}. Forming $F V^{-1}$ and taking its dominant eigenvalue gives $R_0 = \beta N /$ {{2}}. This equals the plain SIR value because every exposed individual eventually becomes {{3}}.
questions.explanation: The determinant is $\det V = k\gamma$. Computing $F V^{-1}$ gives a matrix with dominant eigenvalue $\frac{\beta N}{\gamma}$, so $R_0 = \beta N/\gamma$. It matches SIR because progression through the exposed class is certain: every exposed individual eventually becomes infectious.
questions.source: in the style of Hethcote / Brauer and Castillo-Chavez, Mathematical Models in Population Biology and Epidemiology

questions.stem: Interpret how parameters move the reproduction number.
questions.solution: In $R_0 = \dfrac{\beta N}{\gamma}$, increasing the transmission rate $\beta$ {{1}} the reproduction number, while increasing the recovery rate $\gamma$ {{2}} it. For a disease with contact rate $0.2$ per day and an infectious period of $10$ days, $R_0 =$ {{3}}.
questions.explanation: Since $\beta$ is in the numerator, raising it raises $R_0$; since $\gamma$ is in the denominator, raising it lowers $R_0$. Treating $R_0$ as contact rate times infectious period gives $0.2 \times 10 = 2$.
questions.source: in the style of Hethcote / Brauer and Castillo-Chavez, Mathematical Models in Population Biology and Epidemiology
